metadata.seed: Yeah, there's, like, my main friends that. Yeah, they kind of. It's like peer pressure, sort of. But I can't say no. I feel like I have to say yes just. Just because they are my friends. I do care about them and I don't want to lose them. But sometimes some of the stuff that we do, I feel like I don't really want to do it. And to keep them, I feel like I need to make the sacrifice to do what they want to do and go along with it, even though I know it might not be right. So I just kind of want to build up my self esteem and my confidence and accepting myself. That way I can tell them no and they won't overreact. So I want to be able to say it in a good way. That way I can keep them.

patient.query: Yeah, there's, like, my main friends that. Yeah, they kind of. It's like peer pressure, sort of. But I can't say no. I feel like I have to say yes just. Just because they are my friends. I do care about them and I don't want to lose them. But sometimes some of the stuff that we do, I feel like I don't really want to do it. And to keep them, I feel like I need to make the sacrifice to do what they want to do and go along with it, even though I know it might not be right. So I just kind of want to build up my self esteem and my confidence and accepting myself. That way I can tell them no and they won't overreact. So I want to be able to say it in a good way. That way I can keep them.

automatic_thoughts: I don't want to lose them

neutral: Fear of being a burden to others (finding)
Anxiety about saying the wrong thing (finding)
Fear of being in a small group (finding)

llm_response.response: It sounds like you feel a lot of pressure to say yes to keep your friends. What evidence do you have that saying no would actually lead to losing them? How might you test that idea in a low‑stakes way? What could happen if you tried saying no in a gentle, honest way?

patient.query: I guess I just always worry that if I say no, they’ll think I don’t care or I’m not fun to be around. It’s like… I have this fear that they’ll just stop inviting me altogether. Honestly, just the thought of it makes me feel really anxious. I don’t know if I could handle feeling left out.

neutral: Fear of being in a small group (finding)

llm_response.response: It sounds like you’re worried that saying no will make people think you’re not caring or fun. What evidence have you seen that this actually happens when you decline an invitation? Have there been times when you said no and the group still invited you again?

retrieved_concepts_used.concept: automatic thoughts about being left out
retrieved_concepts_used.label: unknown

patient.query: I mean, I can’t really think of specific times when it happened, but... it’s just this voice in my head that always says, “What if?” It feels like if I say no, I’m risking something. It’s hard to shake that feeling, you know? Like, maybe I’m just overthinking it, but it’s like this shadow that follows me whenever I get an invitation.

automatic_thoughts: If I say no, I'm risking something

entailment: Fear of being left alone during period of dependence (finding)
Fear of saying the wrong thing (finding)

llm_response.response: Have you noticed any times when saying no didn’t lead to the outcome you feared? What evidence supports the idea that refusing an invitation will cause something bad? If you could imagine a friend in the same spot, what advice might you give them? Exploring these angles might help you see other possibilities.

retrieved_concepts_used.concept: fear of saying the wrong thing
retrieved_concepts_used.label: entailment

patient.query: Yeah, I guess there have been times when I didn’t go out and it was fine. Like, my friends didn’t just drop me or anything... but it didn’t feel like that in the moment. I just remember feeling really guilty for saying no, like I’m being rude or something. If it were a friend, I’d probably tell them it’s okay to take time for themselves. But when it’s me, it’s different. I can never seem to give myself that same kindness

llm_response.response: It sounds like you feel a lot of guilt when you decline invitations, as if you’re being rude. What evidence supports that idea, and what evidence might contradict it? How could you extend the same kindness to yourself that you would offer a friend in that situation?

patient.query:
It’s... hard to see evidence on both sides, I guess. I mean, I know my friends have their own lives and understand if I can’t make it sometimes. But it’s like, even if I know that, I can’t shake this feeling that I’m letting them down. I feel like I should be available all the time, like that’s what a good friend does. 

As for being kind to myself, I really don’t know how to do that. I always feel

automatic_thoughts: I should be available all the time
that's what a good friend does

neutral: Fear of being a burden to others (finding)

llm_response.response: It sounds like you’re carrying a lot of pressure about being there for everyone. What kinds of moments or thoughts make you feel you’re falling short? And when you think about being kind to yourself, what might that look like in a small, realistic way?

patient.query:
Yeah, it definitely feels like a lot sometimes. I guess moments when I can’t help out or when I don’t have the energy to join in, it just hits hard. I think about how much they must think I don’t care when, really, I just need a break. It’s like... I imagine them talking behind my back or feeling disappointed.

As for being kind to myself... I mean, I’m not really sure what that looks like. Maybe just letting myself take a day

triggers: moments when I can’t help out
when I don’t have the energy to join in
automatic_thoughts: they must think I don’t care
I imagine them talking behind my back

llm_response.response: I hear how hard it feels when you’re not able to help out and worry about how others might see you. What would it look like for you to treat yourself with the same care you’d offer a friend in that spot? How might you reframe the idea that you need a break as a sign of self‑respect rather than a sign of not caring?

patient.query:
I don’t know... I guess if it were a friend, I’d tell them it’s totally okay to take time for themselves, that they deserve it. I’d remind them that needing a break doesn’t mean they care any less. But when I think about it for myself, it feels selfish. Like I should just push through, because everyone else is doing it. 

Reframing it as self-respect sounds good in theory, but it’s hard to really believe that. I mean

automatic_thoughts: It feels selfish to take a break

neutral: Fear of being left alone during period of dependence (finding)
Carer aware of patient's feelings about dying (finding)

llm_response.response: It sounds like you’re wrestling with a strong sense that taking a break feels like a betrayal of your own responsibilities. What evidence do you have that stepping back is selfish, and how might you view the same situation if you were the friend you’d support? If you could give yourself the same permission you’d offer a friend, what might that look like for you right now?

patient.query:
Yeah, it does feel like I’m betraying something, like I’m not living up to expectations. I don’t really have hard evidence that it’s selfish, it just feels that way in my gut. I guess if I were my friend, I would say it’s okay to rest and that taking care of yourself is actually important for being there for others. 

If I could give myself that same permission... I don’t know, maybe I’d let myself take a quiet day, just relax

automatic_thoughts: I’m not living up to expectations
I would say it’s okay to rest
Taking care of yourself is important for being there for others
Maybe I’d let myself take a quiet day

llm_response.response: It sounds like you’re carrying a lot of weight on yourself. What evidence do you have that taking a quiet day would be a betrayal, and what evidence might suggest it’s actually a healthy choice? How might a trusted friend view this situation differently?

patient.query:
Yeah, it does feel heavy sometimes. I guess I keep thinking about how my friends might see me as flaky or not committed if I bail. But then again, I know they also have their moments when they need space. Maybe they’d understand better than I think. 

If I really think about it, maybe a quiet day could help me recharge, and I’d be better company next time. It’s just hard to shake off that nagging feeling that it’s wrong to prioritize my own needs

automatic_thoughts: friends might see me as flaky
they’d understand better than I think
it's wrong to prioritize my own needs

llm_response.response: It sounds like you’re feeling torn between wanting to be there for your friends and needing a break for yourself. What evidence do you have that taking a quiet day would make you seem flaky? How might your friends respond if you shared that you just need some time to recharge?

retrieved_concepts_used.concept: anxiety about being seen as flaky
retrieved_concepts_used.label: emotions

patient.query:
I mean, I guess I don’t have any real evidence. It’s just this fear that pops up in my mind, like a little voice saying, “What if they think you’re not really into the friendship?” And then I imagine them talking about me, wondering why I’m not coming around as much. 

But thinking about it, my friends are pretty understanding. They talk about needing their own time too, so maybe if I said something, they’d actually support me. It’s just

automatic_thoughts: What if they think you’re not really into the friendship?
They’d actually support me if I said something

llm_response.response:
What clues or experiences lead you to believe that your friends might think you’re not invested in the friendship?  
What signs or conversations have you noticed that suggest they’re actually understanding and supportive?  
How might you test these ideas by sharing your feelings with them or observing their reactions?
